title: Type Checking and Conversion
description: Check the type of an object and convert between types.

slides.title: Checking Types 🔍
slides.content:
R provides several functions to check the type or class of an object:

* `class()` – high-level class (e.g., "numeric", "character", "data.frame")
* `typeof()` – internal type (e.g., "double", "integer", "character")
* `is.*()` family – e.g., `is.numeric()`, `is.character()`, `is.logical()`, `is.na()`

```r
x <- 3.14
class(x)     # "numeric"
typeof(x)    # "double"
is.numeric(x) # TRUE
is.integer(x) # FALSE
```

slides.title: Check Types
slides.content: Run this to see type checking in action:
slides.codeSnippet:
a <- 5L
b <- "hello"
c <- TRUE

print(class(a))
print(typeof(a))
print(is.character(b))
print(is.logical(c))

quizData.question: What does `is.numeric(5L)` return?
quizData.explanation: Integers are considered numeric in R, so `is.numeric()` returns TRUE for integers.

slides.title: Type Conversion (Coercion) 🔄
slides.content:
You can convert between types using `as.*()` functions:

* `as.numeric()` – convert to numeric (double)
* `as.integer()` – convert to integer
* `as.character()` – convert to character
* `as.logical()` – convert to logical

## Rules:
* Numeric to logical: 0 -> FALSE, non-zero -> TRUE
* Character to numeric: must be a number, otherwise NA with warning
* Logical to numeric: TRUE -> 1, FALSE -> 0

```r
as.numeric("123")    # 123
as.character(100)     # "100"
as.logical(0)         # FALSE
```

slides.title: Conversion Examples
slides.codeSnippet:
x <- "3.14"
y <- as.numeric(x)
print(y + 1)

z <- 0
print(as.logical(z))

w <- TRUE
print(as.numeric(w))

slides.title: Warning: Invalid Conversion ⚠️
slides.content:
If conversion fails, you get NA with a warning:

```r
as.numeric("hello")  # NA with warning
```

Always check for NA after conversion if you're not sure.

quizData.question: What is the result of `as.logical(c(0, 1, -5))`?
quizData.explanation: 0 becomes FALSE, any non-zero (including negative) becomes TRUE.

slides.title: Practice Conversion
slides.content: Convert the string "3.14" to numeric, add 2, then convert back to character.
slides.codeSnippet:
num_str <- "3.14"
num <- as.numeric(num_str)
num_plus2 <- num + 2
result_char <- as.character(num_plus2)
print(result_char)
